# Style sandbox (do not cite)

This chapter exists purely to experiment with **CSS, layout, figures, tables, math, and citations**.
It should never be cited or linked from the final book.

## Section header (H2)

This is a paragraph of body text. The goal here is to see:

- line height
- paragraph spacing
- bullet styling
- inline emphasis (*italics*, **bold**, `code`)
- links: see [bookdown](https://bookdown.org/)

### Subsection (H3)

More body text. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Sed non risus.
Suspendisse lectus tortor, dignissim sit amet, adipiscing nec, ultricies sed, dolor.

#### Sub-subsection (H4)

Block quotes are important for tone-setting.

> This is a block quote.
>
> It should be visually distinct but not theatrical.
> Think “quiet authority,” not manifesto.

## Figures

```{r test-figure, fig.cap="A deliberately boring plot for CSS testing.", fig.width=5, fig.height=3}
plot(
  x = 1:10,
  y = (1:10)^2,
  type = "b",
  pch = 19,
  main = "Quadratic growth",
  xlab = "x",
  ylab = "x^2"
)
```

## Tables

```{r test-table}
knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Concept = c("Time", "State", "Observability", "Validation"),
    Common_Mistake = c(
      "Treating time as an index",
      "Equating state with measurement",
      "Confusing missingness with latent state",
      "Validating predictions without a target"
    ),
    What_We_Do_Instead = c(
      "Make time first-class",
      "Model latent state explicitly",
      "Separate state from observation",
      "Define validation contracts"
    )
  ),
  caption = "Conceptual contrasts used to test table styling."
)
```

## Math

Inline math should not jump the line height: \( P(Y \mid X) \).

$$
\Pr(T \le t \mid \text{alive at } t_0) = 1 - \exp\left( -\int_{t_0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du \right)
$$

## Code blocks

```r
# What does this look like?
simulate_patient <- function(state, time) {
  if (!state$alive) return(state)
  state$time <- time
  state
}
```

## Citations

Causal reasoning has made explicit what was previously implicit in many statistical workflows [@Pearl2009].
Hierarchical modeling plays a similar clarifying role for multilevel structure [@GelmanHill2006].

## Callouts / asides

::: {.note}
This is a *note* block. It should be visually distinct but not loud.
:::

::: {.warning}
This is a *warning* block. Use sparingly in the real book.
:::

## Final paragraph

End-of-section spacing matters too. This paragraph exists solely to see how the chapter ends visually.
